attrs.alt: Session controls lifecycle — grant controls at sign-in, session controls enforced throughout the session

content.text: Session controls apply after access is granted — they constrain what a user can do within an authenticated session rather than whether they can access the resource at all. Multiple session controls can be active in the same policy, and when multiple policies match, the most restrictive value from each control type wins.
Forces re-authentication after N hours/days or on every sign-in
Prevents or forces 'Stay signed in?' prompt; affects session cookie lifetime
Passes limited/full access claims to the app; app controls download/attach
Apps onboarded to Defender for Cloud Apps proxy
Inline proxy intercepts session; can block upload/download/cut/copy
Enforces strict or disabled mode for real-time token revocation
Prevents Entra ID from issuing extended sessions during outages
Cryptographically binds access token to the device; prevents token theft reuse

Session controls differ from grant controls in timing: grant controls evaluate once at token issuance; session controls are enforced throughout the session — either through token metadata (sign-in frequency, persistent browser), the app itself (app-enforced restrictions), or a proxy (MDCA Conditional Access App Control).

Sign-in frequency forces re-authentication after a configurable time window. Setting it to 'every time' is the most aggressive option and removes any session persistence — the user must authenticate on each browser session start or app launch. Use shorter values for sensitive resources and longer values for high-frequency-use apps where re-auth interrupts productivity.
Sign-in frequency applies per-app, not per-device — a user who re-authenticates for Outlook does not reset the frequency for SharePoint.
'every time' (everyTime frequencyInterval) is more aggressive than '1 hour' — it triggers re-auth even if the user signed in seconds ago in another policy context.
Sign-in frequency interacts with SSPR, PIM activation, and break-glass accounts — test carefully before deploying short intervals to all users.
Tokens issued by non-interactive sign-ins (service principals, daemon apps) are unaffected by sign-in frequency controls.

Persistent browser session controls whether Entra ID presents the 'Stay signed in?' prompt and whether the resulting session cookie persists across browser restarts. Setting 'never' prevents the cookie, so users must authenticate each time they open a new browser session — critical for shared workstations and kiosks.
Pair 'neverPersistent' with a separate policy for managed devices that allows 'always' — this gives corporate laptops reduced authentication friction while unmanaged devices always prompt.

App-enforced restrictions instruct Exchange Online and SharePoint Online to honor a 'limited access' claim from Entra ID. When the claim indicates the device is unmanaged, SharePoint serves files in web-only mode (no downloads), and Exchange blocks email attachment downloads. The CA policy sends the claim; the application enforces the restriction.
App-enforced restrictions only work with Exchange Online and SharePoint Online — other apps do not read these claims.
SharePoint must be separately configured in the SharePoint Admin Center to map the unmanaged-device claim to a limited-access experience.
The restriction is web-browser-only: OneDrive sync clients and desktop Office apps bypass app-enforced restrictions — combine with a compliantDevice grant control to close this gap.
Conditional Access App Control (Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps)

MDCA's Conditional Access App Control routes the browser session through a reverse proxy. The proxy inspects every request and can block uploads, downloads, cut/copy operations, and printing based on DLP policies defined in the MDCA portal. This enables per-session data protection without modifying the application itself.
mcasConfigured is the most flexible type — it delegates enforcement policy to the MDCA access/session policies rather than hard-coding a control in the CA policy.
MDCA proxy only intercepts browser sessions — native desktop apps are not proxied; use grant controls (compliantDevice, compliantApplication) to cover native clients.
App onboarding to MDCA requires the app to support SAML 2.0 or OIDC and the CA policy must route the session to MDCA before the proxy can intercept.
blockDownloads in the CA policy overrides whatever the MDCA portal policy says for that session — use mcasConfigured to keep policy management centralized in MDCA.

Token protection cryptographically binds an access token to the device that requested it using the device's TPM. If an attacker intercepts the token (via AiTM phishing or token theft malware), they cannot replay it on a different device — the service rejects it because the binding proof does not match.

By default, Entra ID can extend session lifetimes beyond the configured sign-in frequency during service outages, preserving user access. Disabling resilience defaults removes this fallback — users cannot authenticate during an Entra ID outage even with an otherwise valid session. Use only in regulated environments with strict 'no exceptions' requirements.

# Disable resilience defaults — prevent extended sessions during Entra ID outages
# By default, during an outage Entra ID can issue sessions lasting beyond the
# configured sign-in frequency as a fallback. Disabling this removes that fallback.

# Use case: regulated environments where no exceptions are permitted
$sessionControls = @{
    DisableResilienceDefaults = $true
}

# Warning: enabling this means users in the middle of an Entra ID outage
# CANNOT authenticate even if their token would normally be valid.
# Confirm with compliance/security team before deploying.

# Check current resilience defaults state on your tenant:
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationFlowsPolicy'
Conflict Resolution When Multiple Policies Apply

When a user's sign-in matches multiple CA policies that each set session controls, Entra ID applies the most restrictive value from each control dimension independently. Understanding this prevents unexpected outcomes from policy overlap.
One policy uses MDCA, other does not
Each app uses its own matching policy frequency

The SigninLogs table records enforcedSessionControls for each CA policy evaluated during sign-in. This lets you confirm that session controls are actually being applied and measure the frequency of sign-in re-auth events triggered by sign-in frequency policies.

// KQL: review which session controls were enforced per policy in the last 24h
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| where ConditionalAccessStatus == 'success'
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where array_length(ConditionalAccessPolicies.enforcedSessionControls) > 0
| extend PolicyName = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName)
| extend SessionControls = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.enforcedSessionControls)
| summarize Count = count() by PolicyName, SessionControls
| order by Count desc

// Find users who triggered sign-in frequency re-auth prompts
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where ResultType == 0
| where AuthenticationRequirement == 'multiFactorAuthentication'
| where ConditionalAccessStatus == 'success'
| where AuthenticationDetails has 'SignInFrequency'
| summarize Reauths = count() by UserPrincipalName
| order by Reauths desc
Session controls constrain behavior during an authenticated session; they do not block access — that is the role of grant controls.
Sign-in frequency forces periodic re-authentication; 'every time' is the most aggressive setting.
Persistent browser controls whether the 'Stay signed in?' cookie survives browser restart — use 'never' for shared or unmanaged devices.
App-enforced restrictions limit SharePoint and Exchange to web-only mode for unmanaged devices, preventing file downloads.
MDCA Conditional Access App Control enables inline proxy enforcement — block uploads, downloads, and clipboard operations — for browser sessions.
Token protection (preview) binds tokens to the originating device's TPM, defeating token-theft replay attacks.
When multiple CA policies match, the most restrictive session control value from each type wins.
